speaker: interviewer
text: '96. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing Beatrix Sachs. We're in London, England. This is tape four. So you were just talking about bringing up your children and-- and the lives that they've achieved for themselves. When you look back on your own life, what are the things that stand out most for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really can't say. I think I live really, I suppose, for them. I mean, I did have husbands, and I made them very happy, but I seem to lose them. I wasn't very happy. I'm not lucky with husbands, am I? And I'm old now, so that's all finished, done-- over and done with. It's now old age that I've got to cope with them and the end-- death, which I'm not frightened. I think I'm quite-- I've always being adventurous, so at last I'll know if there is a heaven or a hell, or nothing, or I won't know. It won't be a problem anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of hobbies and interests have you had in your life, in the last-- recently, more recently.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've been interested in history. I'm very interested in history. And I love music having always had music around me as a young person. And I like the theater. I'm a great reader. I love reading. I could just go on reading for days on end and good books, too. I had a good education, traveled quite a bit. And I suppose looking after my husbands, which I thoroughly enjoyed spoiling them, making them happy. And I was a good lover. They all adored me. So I felt I-- I-- I'd-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was happy, and I didn't feel I missed out on anything in that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any kind of message that you would like to leave your great-grandchildren, let alone your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of the things I've always tried to do is live for the day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Don't look for something better all the time, because you miss-- miss the day that you're living. So live for the day and try and find it in that one day for the moment till the next day comes. It's a good idea, if you can do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and do you feel that you have been able to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, wasn't very difficult for me because I've got an optimistic nature. I always think, well, something-- something good will turn-- something else will turn up. I'm always thinking like that. I'm lucky that I don't have to sort of force myself to do things because it comes naturally. And I think it's-- I've got an optimistic nature, thanks to whoever parent left me that bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we finish, is there anything else that you would like to add, anything that's been missed, or anything else you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I sometimes feel that I've been rather hard done by, that I've always been a good wife, and why did I have to always lose my husbands? Something I could never understand why my fate should be that, because I-- I always considered that my career was being a very good wife. I won't say I was a good mother, because I didn't think I was a good mother. I wasn't patient enough. I was impatient. But my husbands always adored me. I was lucky in that way, but it was always short-lived by-- by circumstances, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not by neglect or anything, just history. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sachs, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: This letter is written to Nicolas Frank, Beatrix Sachs's oldest-- eldest son, although there are copies of the letter to all-- to herself and to her three sons. She got this letter because she was under sort of special treatment because of her husband, Leo Frank's, eminent position. And the letter says that they have decided that they-- and agreed that they do not want to travel to Amsterdam or to a work camp in another country.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Ausweiskarte, the exit card, for the camp of Lager Westerbork, for Beatrix Frank-Van Lier, issued 29 of September, 1943. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is an Arbeitsausweis, a work pass for Beatrix Frank in the ghetto of Theresienstadt. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the Essenkarte from Theresienstadt for the month of June, the food ticket. This one particularly is for Steven Frank, but there were others issued for the other members of the family, too. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Here are two different versions of the yellow star. The top one, its says Jude, Jew in German, is attached to a book that Beatrix keeps various documents and letters that she has collected from her time during the war. The lower star, in a slightly different yellow, is in Dutch. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] On the left is a booklet for the bank of the-- Jewish bank in Theresienstadt. The dates inside-- written inside are all 1945 dates. On the right, various different denominations of money used in Theresienstadt. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This black album is full of autographs and signatures of musicians and well-known people who Beatrix-- whose autographs Beatrix accumulated in her early childhood, and she carried this book with her throughout her time during the war-- through Westerbork, through Theresienstadt, hid it under mattresses, and has treasured it ever since. And it has inside it, the autographs of people such as Arthur Conan Doyle, Richard Strauss. This is Arthur Conan Doyle's signature. This is an invitation to a supper given to meet Dr. Richard Strauss on March 12, 1910. Actually, the year that you-- that Beatrix was born, before she was born. On the back is Richard Strauss' signature and Thomas Beecham. Oh, really, Thomas Beecham on there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother was a very talented violinist in her own right and had her own ladies' orchestra called the Windsor Ladies' Orchestra. In England, they used to give concerts. And she was very beautiful and charming. And then my father fell in love with her, and they got married, and she joined his orchestra in Eastbourne in the Grand Hotel, and played there for many, many years, about 20 years in his-- in the orchestra in Eastbourne. Till they moved to London, and my father became the publisher at Keith Brauss, music publisher. Published music for the firm of Keith Brauss. Yes, this is a cartoon of my father in Eastbourne, who was quite a well-known personality there. Yeah, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a very good violinist, and left many happy memories of his concerts and dances he used-- his orchestra used to play for in Eastbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a photograph of Beatrix's second son, Steven, with one of his younger children. We think it's Simon. You have three sons and an adopted daughter, and how many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one great grand--

speaker: interviewee
text: And one great grandson. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a letter written to Beatrix from a lady who was called-- whose camp name was Sister Rosemary. And it fills in some of the gaps from the videotape, so we're going to scan through it. [PAUSES FOR 157 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Shall I make a cup of coffee for anybody? What about that?

speaker: interviewer
text: In a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Coffee?

speaker: interviewer
text: In a minute.
